name: anyhow
what_it_does: Flexible error handling library for Rust applications. Provides anyhow::Error as a single error type that wraps any std::error::Error implementation, with context attachment and downcasting. The ? operator chains errors automatically. Designed for application code (not libraries) where you want easy error propagation with context, not fine-grained error type discrimination.
use_cases: Propagate errors in agent application code with .context("doing X") to build error chains that explain what the agent was doing
Simplify error handling in agent main() functions with anyhow::Result<()> instead of custom error types
Collect errors from multiple sources (network, file I/O, JSON parsing) into a single Result type without wrapping
Add contextual error messages to LLM API failures to trace which agent step caused the error
Prototype agent error handling quickly before moving to typed thiserror errors in library code
not_for: Library crates — use thiserror for typed errors that callers can match on; anyhow loses type information
Applications needing to programmatically match error types — use thiserror enums for recoverable error handling
best_when: You're writing Rust application code (not library code) and want ergonomic error propagation with rich context without defining custom error types.
avoid_when: You're writing a library crate — use thiserror to expose typed errors that library consumers can match and handle programmatically.

auth.notes: Local error handling library — no authentication required.

pricing.notes: MIT/Apache-2.0 dual license. Written by David Tolnay, author of serde and many other foundational Rust crates.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — local error handling with minimal overhead.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Error display chains context messages clearly. {:?} debug format shows full backtrace (if RUST_BACKTRACE=1). .context() strings appear in error chain in order of wrapping.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Error handling library — pure functional, no side effects.
known_agent_gotchas: anyhow::Error erases the original error type — you cannot match error variants; use thiserror if you need to recover from specific error kinds
Backtrace capture requires RUST_BACKTRACE=1 environment variable at runtime — not captured by default for performance; set in development but consider the overhead in production
.with_context(|| format!(...)) is lazy (closure) while .context("string") is eager — use with_context for expensive string formatting that should only run on error
anyhow does not implement std::error::Error itself (by design) — you cannot use ? to propagate anyhow::Error into a Box<dyn Error> result in library code
In library crates, returning anyhow::Error forces consumers to add anyhow as a dependency just to use your crate — always use thiserror in library code
downcast_ref::<T>() can recover original error types from anyhow::Error — useful when consuming APIs that return anyhow errors and you need to handle specific cases

security_score_components.security_notes: Error handling library. Be careful not to include secrets in error context strings — error messages may be logged. No network calls.
